description.en: Mallorca's signature spiral: a lard-laminated dough (saïm gives it its name) stretched paper-thin, coiled flat like a snail shell, and dusted white with powdered sugar. Once festival fare, it's now an everyday breakfast and merienda treat.
description.de: Mallorcas Wahrzeichen unter dem Gebäck: ein mit Schweineschmalz (saïm) laminierter Teig, hauchdünn gezogen, flach zur Schnecke aufgerollt und mit Puderzucker bestäubt. Einst Festtagsgebäck, heute alltägliches Frühstück und Vesper.
description.es: El emblema dulce de Mallorca: una masa laminada con manteca de cerdo (el saïm que le da nombre), estirada finísima, enrollada en espiral plana como una caracola y cubierta de azúcar glas. Antes solo en fiestas, hoy desayuno y merienda de cada día.
description.fr: L'emblème sucré de Majorque : une pâte laminée au saindoux (le saïm qui lui donne son nom), étirée très finement, enroulée à plat en spirale et saupoudrée de sucre glace. Autrefois réservée aux fêtes, on la déguste aujourd'hui au petit-déjeuner ou au goûter.

origin.note: Protected Geographical Indication tied specifically to Mallorca since at least the 17th century; the name comes straight from saïm, Mallorquín for pork lard, the fat that's stretched through the dough rather than kneaded into it.

ingredients.note: 60 g in an overnight poolish, the rest in the final dough
60 g in the poolish, the rest in the dough
a trace in the poolish, the rest in the dough
fresh pork lard, stretched through the dough during lamination

label.en: candied winter squash filling (cabello de ángel)

ingredients.note: optional filling for one of the two coils; omit for the plain version

yield.shape: two flat coiled spirals, about 22 cm across

keeping.note: Best very fresh, within a day while the coil stays light and delicate; wrap airtight for up to two days, or freeze whole and warm briefly before serving.

method.title: Build the poolish
method.summary: The day before, stir a little flour, water and a little yeast into a loose batter and let it ferment slowly overnight, about 9 hours at cool room temperature, until flat and bubbly.

method.title: Mix and knead the dough
method.summary: Work the poolish into the remaining flour with the eggs, sugar, water and yeast, kneading at length until the gluten is fully developed, then knead in the olive oil. Rest the dough, covered, about an hour.

method.title: Stretch and laminate with lard
method.summary: Roll one portion of dough out, then stretch it by hand across a table until almost see-through. Smear the surface generously with soft lard, right out to the edges.

method.title: Roll and coil
method.summary: Add any filling in a line across the stretched dough, roll it up into a tight cylinder, then coil it loosely into a flat spiral, leaving room to expand. Repeat with the second portion.

method.title: Long, slow fermentation
method.summary: Let the coiled dough ferment loosely covered for about 20 hours at cool room temperature, then the next day gently re-stretch and re-coil it onto a lined tray.

method.title: Final proof
method.summary: Let the coils proof somewhere warm until roughly tripled in volume, which can take up to 14 hours.

method.title: Bake and finish
method.summary: Bake at around 180°C for 18-20 minutes until golden, cool completely, then dust generously with powdered sugar just before serving.

source.adaptation: Lard amount (150 g) is estimated/composited from several traditional recipes, since this source gives only a 'generous' smear rather than a gram figure; formula confidence marked estimated.
